data.name: Classical Ciphers
data.description: Explore historical encryption methods including Caesar, Vigenere, Playfair, Beaufort, Vernam, Bacon, Atbash, A1Z26, and other classical ciphers. Encrypt, decrypt, and learn how traditional cryptography works online.
data.meta_description: Learn and use classical ciphers online including Caesar, Vigenere, Playfair, Vernam, Bacon, Atbash, Beaufort, Gronsfeld, and A1Z26.

data.title: What are classical ciphers?
data.text:
Classical ciphers are historical encryption methods developed long before modern computer cryptography. They transform messages using substitution, transposition, keywords, or simple mathematical rules to conceal information from unintended readers.

Although these algorithms are no longer considered secure for real-world protection, they remain important for learning the foundations of cryptography, understanding cipher design, and exploring the history of secret communication.

data.title: How to choose the right cipher
data.text:
Different classical ciphers serve different educational purposes. Caesar and Atbash are ideal for learning basic substitution techniques. Vigenere, Beaufort, and Gronsfeld introduce keyword-based and polyalphabetic encryption. Playfair demonstrates digraph substitution, while Vernam introduces XOR-based encryption concepts.

Bacon focuses on steganography and hidden messages, whereas A1Z26 is commonly used in puzzles, treasure hunts, and educational activities. Choose the cipher that best matches the concept you want to explore.

data.title: Substitution, polyalphabetic, and steganographic methods
data.text:
Classical ciphers can be divided into several groups. Simple substitution ciphers such as Caesar and Atbash replace characters using fixed rules. Polyalphabetic ciphers such as Vigenere, Beaufort, and Gronsfeld use changing substitutions controlled by a key.

Other systems explore different ideas. Playfair encrypts pairs of letters, Vernam introduces XOR operations and One-Time Pad concepts, while Bacon hides messages inside seemingly innocent text using steganography.

data.title: History of Classical Cryptography
data.text:
The history of cryptography dates back thousands of years. Early civilizations used simple substitution methods to conceal military, political, and diplomatic communications. One of the best-known examples is the Caesar Cipher, traditionally associated with Julius Caesar and his military correspondence.

During the Renaissance, more sophisticated systems emerged. Polyalphabetic ciphers such as Vigenere and Beaufort were developed to resist simple frequency analysis and remained in use for centuries. Other methods explored different approaches, including digraph substitution, numeric keys, and steganography.

Although classical ciphers have been replaced by modern cryptographic algorithms, they remain valuable for understanding how encryption evolved and how many core concepts of modern cryptography originated.

data.title: Encrypt text with a custom shift
data.description: Use Caesar Cipher to apply a configurable letter shift and learn the basics of substitution encryption.

data.title: Protect messages with a keyword
data.description: Use Vigenere Cipher to encrypt text with repeating keyword-based shifts.

data.title: Hide messages inside ordinary text
data.description: Use Bacon Cipher steganography mode to conceal information within another message.

data.title: Experiment with XOR encryption
data.description: Explore Vernam Cipher and One-Time Pad concepts using random or custom keys.

data.question: Are classical ciphers secure today?
data.answer: Most classical ciphers can be broken quickly using modern computers and cryptanalysis techniques. They are primarily used for education, puzzles, and historical study.

data.question: What is the difference between Caesar and Vigenere?
data.answer: Caesar uses a single fixed shift for every letter, while Vigenere uses a keyword to apply multiple shifting alphabets throughout the message.

data.question: Which classical cipher is best for beginners?
data.answer: Caesar Cipher and Atbash are usually the easiest starting points because they use simple substitution rules and require little setup.

data.question: What is a polyalphabetic cipher?
data.answer: A polyalphabetic cipher uses multiple substitution alphabets instead of a single fixed mapping. Vigenere, Beaufort, and Gronsfeld are common examples.

data.question: What is steganography?
data.answer: Steganography hides the existence of a message rather than simply encrypting it. The Bacon Cipher is a historical example of a steganographic method.

data.question: What can I learn from classical ciphers?
data.answer: Classical ciphers help explain substitution, transposition, key management, frequency analysis, steganography, and many other concepts that influenced modern cryptography.

notes: Сохраняйте id у каждой сущности без изменений.
Менять нужно только текстовые поля в data.
Пустые строки допустимы: при импорте это удалит перевод для конкретного языка.
Новые элементы без id или с id=0 разрешено добавлять только в файле где meta.language == meta.default_language.
Для новых tasks и used_together указывайте alias связанных шифров.
